coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective sensing of copper ion and sulfide anion in lake water
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Jia
Shen, Yanmei
Wang, Weixia
Li, Yuqing
Yang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Water contamination by copper ions (Cu2+) and sulfide anions (S2-) has been a longstanding human health hazard. The limitations of current detection strategies have created a high demand for effectively monitoring the concentrations of these ions in water systems. A facile fluorescent bioassay for Cu2+ and S2- was developed in this study based on “on-off-on” fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). N-CDs were synthesized for the first time via thermal treatment using hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate precursors. The obtained CDs were highly stable in aqueous solution, with a strong green emission and a 51.2% quantum yield. The maximum fluorescence emission peak was observed at 478 nm for a 360-nm excitation. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs can be quenched by a factor of 6 by Cu2+, which can be applied to Cu2+ assays ranging from 0.05 to 5 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25 nM. Interestingly, further addition of S2- recovered the fluorescence of the N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex, which can be used to quantify S2- in a broad range from 0.05 to 10 μM with an LOD of 32 nM. The N-CD and N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplexes were also demonstrated to be highly specific towards Cu2+ and S2-, respectively. Finally, the sensor was successfully applied to lake water for detecting both Cu2+ and S2-, demonstrating excellent application prospects for analytical chemistry and environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped
Carbon dots
Copper ion
Sulfide anion
Fluorescence sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection by N-CDs of Cu2+ ions
Detection of Cu2+ ion in real samples
Using N-CDs@Cu2+ complex for S2- anion detection
Detection of S2- anions in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21505122 ) and the Science and Technology Plan Project (grant number 2019YFS0335 ), Sichuan Province, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Typical TEM image of the prepared N-CDs in water. (B) Particle size distribution of the N-CDs. (C) XRD patterns of the N-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of the N-CDs.
High resolution XPS spectra of (A) full range, (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s, and (D) O 1 s peak of the N-CDs.

(A) Excitation spectrum of the N-CDs at the 478 nm emission wavelength, and emission spectrum of them at 360 nm excitation wavelength. (B) Fluorescence emission of the N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
The fluorescence emission spectra of different sensing systems: (a) N-CDs; (b) N-CDs @Cu2+ nanocomplex with S2−; (c) N-CDs with Cu2+.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the N-doped carbon dots in the presence of increasing amounts of Cu2+ ion. The arrow indicates the signal changes with Cu2+ ion increasing (from bottom to top, 0, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, and 10 μM). (B) Calibration curve of the assay system for Cu2+ ion detection.
Selectivity test of the N-CDs based fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ assay. The concentration of Cu2+ is 5 μM and all others of different metal ions are 20 μM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs @Cu2+ nanocomplex in the presence of increasing amounts of S2− anion. The arrow indicates the signal changes with S2− anion increasing (from bottom to top, 0, 0.05, 0.5, 1, 5, 7.5, 10, and 15 μM). (B) Calibration curve of the assay system for S2− detection.
Selectivity test of the N-CDs @Cu2+ complex based fluorescence sensor for S2− assay. The concentration of S2− is 10 μM and all others of different anions are 20 μM.
Schematic illustration of the N-doped carbon dots for Cu2+ ion and S2- anion selective detection.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of Cu2+ ion.

row.entry: N-CQDs from citric acid and histidine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The concentration of Cu2+ in water samples detected using the proposed assay.

row.entry: Proposed method mean a ± SD b /nM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different methods for the determination of S2- anion.
The concentration of S2- anion in water samples detected using the proposed assay.

row.entry: Proposed method mean a ± SD b /nM

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective sensing of copper ion and sulfide anion in lake water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Waterloo Institute for Nanotechnology, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON N2L 3G1, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Waterloo Institute for Nanotechnology, University of Waterloo

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Waterloo Institute for Nanotechnology, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, N2L 3G1, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water contamination by copper ions (Cu2+) and sulfide anions (S2-) has been a longstanding human health hazard. The limitations of current detection strategies have created a high demand for effectively monitoring the concentrations of these ions in water systems. A facile fluorescent bioassay for Cu2+ and S2- was developed in this study based on “on-off-on” fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). N-CDs were synthesized for the first time via thermal treatment using hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate precursors. The obtained CDs were highly stable in aqueous solution, with a strong green emission and a 51.2% quantum yield. The maximum fluorescence emission peak was observed at 478 nm for a 360-nm excitation. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs can be quenched by a factor of 6 by Cu2+, which can be applied to Cu2+ assays ranging from 0.05 to 5 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25 nM. Interestingly, further addition of S2- recovered the fluorescence of the N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex, which can be used to quantify S2- in a broad range from 0.05 to 10 μM with an LOD of 32 nM. The N-CD and N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplexes were also demonstrated to be highly specific towards Cu2+ and S2-, respectively. Finally, the sensor was successfully applied to lake water for detecting both Cu2+ and S2-, demonstrating excellent application prospects for analytical chemistry and environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials possess useful physical and chemical properties: reliable synthetic methods have been used to prepare a variety of these materials for successful application to various practical areas [1–3]. Over the past two decades, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively studied as a novel nanomaterial with unique optical and chemical properties, good water solubility, high chemical stability, high quantum yield, superior photostability, environmental friendliness, tunable photoluminescence, and low toxicity [4–6]. A wide range of applications have been demonstrated for fluorescent carbon dots, such as biolabeling, catalysis, biomedical drug detection, energy sources, bioimaging, and sensors [7–9]. Nitrogen (N) is one of the most popular atoms used to dope CDs to promote fluorescence. Many preparation methods for fluorescent N-CDs have been reported, such as ultrasonic-assisted, microplasma-assisted, and hydrothermal techniques, as well as carbonization [10–12]. However, most of these methods have relatively low fluorescent quantum yields and require complex surface modifications to realize various functionalities, which is problematic for practical applications.

Environmental pollution by heavy metal ions in recent years has become one of the main hazards to human health, because the accumulation of these metals in the human body can inhibit normal human physiological and metabolic activity [13–15]. Copper ions (Cu2+) are an essential transition metal for human beings, playing a pivotal role in various physiological processes, such as signal transduction, energy generation, and dioxygen transport [16–18]; however, excessive cellular copper levels have been implicated in Alzheimer's disease, cancer, liver damage, and Wilson's disease [19–21]. The sulfide anion (S2-) is a traditional toxic pollutant found in natural water and wastewater that can be extremely toxic to living organisms [22]. Diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease, Down’s syndrome, and liver cirrhosis, have been related to abnormal levels of S2- in drinking water [23,24]. Considering the widespread industrial use of copper and sulfides, contamination by Cu2+ ions and S2- anions is an environmental problem that requires urgent solution. Therefore, it is very important to detect these ions both separately and simultaneously. Several detection methods have been reported to date, such as titration, chromatography, colorimetry, spectroscopy and electrochemistry [25,26]. However, these methods are time-consuming, relatively costly and have both limited selectivity and sensitivity.

Fluorescence techniques have been extensively used for sensing analytes because of operational simplicity, a rapid response, and high sensitivity [27,28]. Various fluorescent probes, such as organic molecules [29], quantum dots [30], and metal nanoclusters [31], have been successfully used to sense Cu2+. A wide variety of organic molecule fluorescent probes with high sensitivity and adaptability have been used to detect H2S [32]. However, the abovementioned fluorescent methods usually suffer from limitations, such as low sensitivity and selectivity, poor photostability, relatively high toxicity, and/or high cost. Therefore, it extremely important to develop a novel strategy with low cost, high sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ ions and S2- anion assays to solve the abovementioned problems. CDs possess unique optical and chemical properties, good water solubility, high chemical stability and environmental friendliness and could thus be used as a novel fluorescent nanoprobe as an alternative to organic dyes, heavy metal-based quantum dots, and metal nanoclusters.

We present the first preparation of N-CDs via thermal treatment using a simple strategy and hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate precursors ( Scheme 1). In this system, amine was obtained through the high-temperature pyrolysis of hexamethylene-tetramine. Ammonium citrate contains ammonium, hydroxyl and multiple carboxyl groups and provided O, N and C sources. The carboxyl groups of citric acid polymerized with amine or hydroxyl groups to form amides and esters, which further polymerized and carbonized into polymeric networks and carbon nuclei. The polymer carbon dots were subsequently carbonized to produce CDs [33,34]. The fluorescence of N-CDs can be directly quenched by Cu2+ ions (by a factor of 6), and the fluorescence of the resulting N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex can be restored by the addition of S2- anions. The N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The fluorescence emission of the synthesized CDs was recorded with a spectrofluorometer, and the quantum yield was calculated. Then, the N-CD and N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplexes were both employed as nanoprobes to detect Cu2+ ions and S2- anions by “on-off-on” fluorescence, respectively. The developed sensors were systematically characterized in lake water (as well as ultrapure water) to demonstrate their application prospects for analytical chemistry and environmental remediation.

Hexamethylene-tetramine, ammonium citrate, Na2S, sodium salts of anions (Cl−, CO3 2−, Br−, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −) and chloride salts of cations (Mn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All reagents were of analytical grade and were used directly without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

N-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate as precursors. The procedure consisted of adding 0.1 g of ammonium citrate and 0.058 g of hexamethylene-tetramine to 20 mL of ultrapure water, followed by transferring the mixture to an autoclave and heating at 180 ℃ for 6 h. N-CD solutions were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove carbide residues. The resulting dark brown solution was further purified by an ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (molecular weights: 3000 Da) [35,36], freeze-dried to obtain a solid powder and diluted with ultrapure water for further use.

We also have purified carbon dots solution via silica column chromatography with fused-in frit with a length of 200 mm and a volume of 35 mL [37,38]. The silica gel stationary phase material had a mean pore size of 15 nm. The eluent for the fractionation was petroleum ether: ethyl acetate with a vol. ratio of 1:1. Afterward, the collected N-CDs with blue fluorescence were treated to remove solvents by rotary evaporation and dispersed in water. The optical property is consistent with the N-CDs purified by an ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (molecular weights: 3000 Da).

In a typical assay, 10 μL of an aqueous solution of N-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) was added to 170 μL of PB (100 mM, pH 7.4), followed by addition of 20 μL of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a F-4600 fluorometer after 10 min of reaction. The selectivity of Cu2+ ion sensing was confirmed by adding other common metal ions (including Mn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Co2+ ions) and carrying out the same detection procedure.

Ten microliters of a N-CD solution (0.25 mg/mL), 20 μL of a solution of Cu2+ ions (50 μM), and 160 μL of a PB solution (100 mM, pH 7.4) were mixed and allowed to stand for 5 min to form N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplexes. Then, 10 μL of different concentrations of Na2S were added to the abovementioned mixture at room temperature. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a F-4600 fluorometer after reaction for 10 min at room temperature. The selectivity of the N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex towards S2- was investigated by adding a series of sodium salts of anions (NO2 −, NO3 −, Cl−, Br−, I−, and CO3 2−) to the detection system.

N-CDs were synthesized at a yield of 21.8% by thermal treatment using hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate precursors. The morphology of the resulting N-CDs was characterized by TEM. The TEM images showed that the N-CDs were almost spherical ( Fig. 1A) and fairly monodisperse with an average size of approximately 3.22 nm (Fig. 1B). X-ray diffraction analysis revealed poor crystallinity for the prepared N-CDs (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1D shows the FT-IR spectrum: the broad absorption band at 3431 cm−1 was assigned to -OH or -NH2, which promote the water solubility of N-CDs [39]. The absorption peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1524 cm−1 were associated with C˭C stretching vibrations and C-O bending vibrations, respectively. The appearance of absorption peaks at 2972 cm−1 and 1093 cm−1 indicated the formation of C-H and C-O groups, respectively [40]. The FT-IR analysis indicated that the surface of the N-CDs was passivated by both oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups. The components of the prepared N-CDs were determined by XPS ( Fig. 2). The full XPS survey spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2A. The results showed that the N-CDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The XPS survey spectrum of the N-CDs exhibited three major peaks at 286.6, 400.2, and 532.9 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. 2A). The atomic percentages of elemental C, N, and O were calculated to be 78.78%, 3.16%, and 14.19%, respectively. Deconvolution of the C 1 s spectra resulted in three peaks at 283.9, 285.4, and 287.8 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N, and C˭O, respectively (Fig. 2B). Deconvolution of the N 1 s spectra resulted in two peaks at 399.2 and 400.7 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and graphitic N bonds (Fig. 2C) [41]. This result confirmed the doping of nitrogen atoms into the CDs. The nitrogen-containing functional groups enhanced the conjugation degree of the N-CDs, thereby increasing the electron transition probability and contributing to the high quantum yield of the N-CDs. The deconvoluted spectrum of O 1 s exhibited two components at 531.5, 532.7, and 534.5 that were attributed to C-O-H, C-O-C, and O˭C‒O, respectively (Fig. 2D). The EDS analysis showed that the N-CDs were composed of C, N, and O (Fig. S1), which was consistent with the XPS results. All these results provided evidence that the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups in the N-CDs improved the water solubility of the N-CDs and modified the surfaces of the N-CDs to promote interaction with metal ions.

Fig. 3 A shows that the highest fluorescent emission peak for the N-CDs was obtained at 478 nm under a maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited blue emission with a high quantum yield (51.2%) with dansyl chloride as the standard, demonstrating good fluorescence properties. The fluorescence emission spectra under variable excitation wavelengths for N-CDs are shown in Fig. 3B. A redshift in the emission of N-CDs was observed over excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 400 nm, indicating excitation-dependent emission of the N-CDs. This excitation-dependent emission resulted from the size and amino functionalization of the N-CDs, demonstrating tunable fluorescence.

The pH-dependent fluorescence of the N-CDs was investigated. Fig. S2 shows that the fluorescence gradually increased for pHs from 3.0 to 7.4 and decreased for pHs from 8 to 10. The fluorescence of the N-CDs was remarkably stable over a wide pH range. The effect of the NaCl concentration on the fluorescence of the N-CDs was also investigated. The N-CDs were very stable in high-ionic strength NaCl solutions, which is useful for applications under various salt conditions (Fig. S3). Fig. S4 shows a little variation in the fluorescence of the N-CDs after continuous excitation with UV light exposure for 60 min, showing good photostability for the N-CDs. All these features of the N-CDs demonstrate good potential application prospects.

The feasibility of detecting Cu2+ ions and S2- anions for sensing applications was investigated. Fig. 4 shows that the N-CD solution exhibits strong fluorescence under excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 4, curve a). With the addition of Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared N-CDs decreases significantly because of effective electron or energy transfer between Cu2+ ions and N-CDs (Fig. 4, curve c) to form the N-CD@Cu2+ complex. The high affinity of the S2- anion for Cu2+ ions enables the S2- anion to trigger the release of Cu2+ ions from the N-CD@Cu2+ complex to form stable CuS, thereby recovering the fluorescence of the N-CDs (Fig. 4, curve b). The results validate the novel strategy based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence of Cu2+ ions and S2- anions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection by N-CDs of Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction time between the N-CDs and Cu2+ ions was further investigated. Fig. S5 shows that the fluorescence of the N-CDs decreased quickly upon addition of 5 μM Cu2+ ions and to reached equilibrium within 5 min. The fluorescence intensity did not change over the following 30 min. This result indicated rapid quenching of Cu2+ ions for the fluorescent N-CDs, that is, rapid sensing of Cu2+ ions.

Fig. 5 A shows that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs decreased gradually with increasing Cu2+ ion content. Fig. 5B indicates a good linear relationship between the Cu2+ ion concentration (over the 0.05–5 μM range) and the fluorescence intensity given by F = −153.5 C Cu 2+ + 1082.2 with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9968 (where C Cu 2+ denotes the concentration of Cu2+ ions and F denotes the fluorescence). Table 1 shows that the calculated limit of detection (LOD) of Cu2+ ions of 25 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The relative standard deviations in the peak fluorescence readings were 2.6%, 3.8%, and 2.2% for three repetitive assays of 50 nM, 500 nM, and 1000 nM Cu2+ ions, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of Cu2+ was much lower than the maximum Cu2+ concentration in drinking water (2 ppm) recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO). The proposed system provides a simple and sensitive sensor for Cu2+ ion detection (Table 1) among existing approaches. The LOD of the proposed method is comparable to or lower than that of other nanomaterials for Cu2+ ion sensing based on fluorescence methods using materials such as B, N-CQDs (0.3 μM) [42], polymer nanoparticles (62 nM) [43], graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (70 nM) [44], Au nanoclusters (109 nM) [45], or N-CQDs fabricated from citric acid and histidine (0.19 μM) [46]. These results indicate that N-CDs are an effective fluorescence-quenching probe to quantitatively measure Cu2+ ions with a low detection limit for environmental sample analysis.

Selectivity is an important parameter for evaluating sensing system performance. Fig. 6 shows the measured results for various cations, including Mn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Co2+ ions, at a concentration of 20 μM, indicating no discernable fluorescence changes. However, the addition of Cu2+ ions (5 μM) sharply decreased the fluorescence intensity. These results demonstrated that the N-CD-based sensor can be used to selectively detect Cu2+ ions over other metal ions. This high selectivity could be attributed to the variety of amino groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the N-CDs that have a high binding affinity towards Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ ion in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the developed approach was tested by evaluating the sensor for Cu2+ detection in local lakes (Mei Lake) and tap water (from Zhengzhou, China). Water samples were spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (see Table 2). Good recovery rates of Cu2+ ions, ranging from 96.4% to 106.4%, were obtained for the real samples (Table 2). These results demonstrated the potential of the proposed method for detecting Cu2+ ions in practical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using N-CDs@Cu2+ complex for S2- anion detection
ce:section.ce:para: We optimized the sensing performance for S2- anion detection by investigating the effect of the reaction time. Fig. S6 shows the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex in the presence of 10 μM S2- anions. Fluorescence recovery was fairly rapid. The fluorescence reached equilibrium within 10 min and remained stable for longer reaction times. Thus, we chose 10 min as the optimal reaction time for S2- anion detection.

Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different S2- anion concentrations under the optimized experimental conditions. Fig. 7A shows that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex increased gradually with the S2- anion concentration. Fig. 7B indicates a good linear relationship between the S2- anion concentration (over 0.05–10 μM) and the fluorescence intensity given by F = 84.51 C S 2- + 215.3 with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9956 (where C S 2- denotes the S2- anion concentration, and F denotes the fluorescence). The calculated detection limit of the S2- anion (LOD) was 32 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (see Table 3). The relative standard deviations in the peak fluorescence readings were 2.9%, 3.1%, and 2.7% for three repetitive assays of 100 nM, 500 nM, and 1000 nM S2- anions, respectively. The LOD of this proposed method is comparable to or lower than other methods for S2- anion assays, such as those using Cu nanoclusters (42 nM) [47], carbon dots (320 nM) [48], Cu-doped carbon quantum dots (0.5 μM) [49], graphene (25.3 μM) [50], or lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles (510 nM) [22]. The experimental results demonstrated the applicability of the N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex for sensitive S2- anion detection.

The specificity of the sensor towards the S2- anion is as important as the sensitivity. Fig. 8 shows that only the S2- anion (10 μM) induced a significant increase in the fluorescence. Other coexisting anions found in environmental and biological settings, such as Br−, NO3 −, Cl−, I−, NO2 −, and CO3 2−, had almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity, even at a concentration of 20 μM. These anions also did not interfere with the fluorescence detection of the S2- anion. Therefore, the results indicate good selectivity of the N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex for the S2- anion assay because of the high affinity of the S2- anion towards Cu2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S2- anions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: We demonstrated the feasibility of the sensor for S2- anion detection in real samples by using the developed sensor system to measure the S2- anion concentration in local lakes (Mei Lake) and tap water (from Zhengzhou, China). Table 4 shows satisfactory recovery rates of S2- anions in the real samples ranging from 98.0% to 103.4%. These results indicate that our N-CD@Cu2+-nanocomplex-based fluorescent sensor is feasible for S2- anion detection in real samples.

In summary, the N-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using hexamethylene-tetramine and ammonium citrate as precursors. The N-CDs exhibited blue fluorescence at a high quantum yield (51.2%) relative to other reported N-CDs. Cu2+ ions efficiently quenched the fluorescence of N-CDs by forming an N-CD@Cu2+ nanocomplex due to the coordination of Cu2+ ions with amino groups at the surface of the N-CDs. Addition of S2- anions recovered the fluorescence of the N-CDs, because the Cu2+ ions interacted with S2- anions to form CuS. This N-CDs offered two advantages: (1) N atom doped to CDs could improve the optical property of the N-CDs, which is benefit for the sensitive sensing of Cu2+ ion and S2- anion; (2) the amino groups on the surface of the N-CDs have a high binding affinity towards Cu2+, which could be used for the selective sensing of Cu2+ ion. The proposed method is a simple and rapid detection procedure that does not involve laborious chemical syntheses or complex sample pretreatment. The proposed method also offers advantages such as high sensitivity, good selectivity, low cost, and a wide linear response range, and has been applied for the detection of Cu2+ ions and S2- anions in environmental samples. Consequently, this simple fluorescence sensor is feasible for environmental monitoring of Cu2+ ions and S2- anions. The results of this study can be used to advance the development of highly sensitive fluorescence sensors based on N-CDs with wide applications to biosensing, imaging, drug delivery, etc.

Jia Ge: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Yanmei Shen: Investigation. Weixia Wang: Data curation, Software. Yuqing Li: Conceptualization, Methodology. Yu Yang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505122) and the Science and Technology Plan Project (grant number 2019YFS0335), Sichuan Province, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2021.105081.
